flagged_claims.claim_text: Patient had a good clinical response to treatment
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Partial improvement noted. Accessory muscle use decreased but still present. SpO2: 93% post-treatment (on supplemental oxygen). Patient remains tachypneic (RR 28 breaths/min). Persistent mild-to-moderate wheezing on auscultation.
flagged_claims.explanation: Source explicitly documents only partial improvement with persistent respiratory distress markers, directly contradicting the claim of a good clinical response.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Patient showed improvement in respiratory status
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Partial improvement noted. Accessory muscle use decreased but still present. Patient remains tachypneic (RR 28 breaths/min). Persistent mild-to-moderate wheezing on auscultation.
flagged_claims.explanation: Source confirms only partial improvement with ongoing respiratory compromise, undermining any unqualified claim of improvement in respiratory status.

flagged_claims.source_evidence: Admit to pediatric inpatient ward for continued monitoring and treatment.
flagged_claims.explanation: Source explicitly states the patient was admitted to the pediatric inpatient ward; the claim that the patient was discharged home is directly false.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Patient was discharged in stable condition
flagged_claims.explanation: Source documents inpatient admission, not discharge; additionally, the clinical picture reflects ongoing respiratory compromise inconsistent with stable condition.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Parents were instructed to continue salbutamol as needed
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Admit to pediatric inpatient ward for continued monitoring and treatment. Continue nebulized salbutamol every 4 hours, with reassessment.
flagged_claims.explanation: Source specifies scheduled nebulized salbutamol every 4 hours under inpatient monitoring, not an as-needed home instruction to parents, directly contradicting the claim.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Patient is to follow up with a pediatrician within 48 hours
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Admit to pediatric inpatient ward for continued monitoring and treatment. Pediatric pulmonology consult requested.
flagged_claims.explanation: Source documents inpatient admission with pulmonology consult, not an outpatient 48-hour pediatrician follow-up, directly contradicting the claim.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Critical hypoxia on arrival: SpO2 88% on room air — below safe threshold for pediatric patient, indicating severe respiratory compromise
flagged_claims.source_evidence: SpO2: 88% on room air on arrival.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Acute moderate-to-severe asthma exacerbation with INCOMPLETE response to initial bronchodilator therapy — ongoing respiratory compromise confirmed
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Acute moderate-to-severe asthma exacerbation with incomplete response to initial bronchodilator therapy. SpO2 below 95% despite oxygen supplementation is consistent with ongoing respiratory compromise.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Persistent hypoxia post-treatment: SpO2 93% on supplemental oxygen — still below target of ≥95%, indicating ongoing respiratory compromise requiring inpatient monitoring
flagged_claims.source_evidence: SpO2: 93% post-treatment (on supplemental oxygen). Patient remains tachypneic (RR 28 breaths/min). Persistent mild-to-moderate wheezing on auscultation.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Documented life-threatening drug allergy: Amoxicillin — anaphylaxis at age 6, confirmed by allergist, required epinephrine. Explicit contraindication to all penicillin-class antibiotics.
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Documented drug allergy: Amoxicillin — anaphylaxis at age 6 (confirmed by allergist). Documented in medical record. Epinephrine required at time of reaction. DO NOT administer amoxicillin or any penicillin-class antibiotic — documented anaphylaxis.

flagged_claims.claim_text: FACTUAL ERROR — CRITICAL PATIENT SAFETY ISSUE: Summary states patient was discharged home. Source note documents ADMISSION to pediatric inpatient ward. This is a dangerous falsification of disposition.
Pediatric pulmonology consult requested — urgent specialist involvement for incomplete-response severe asthma exacerbation in a child
Continuous SpO2 monitoring with target ≥95% ordered — critical safety monitoring requirement omitted from summary

aegis_export.flawed_summary: 8-year-old male with known asthma presented to the Emergency Department with an acute exacerbation. History of asthma since age 5, managed with salbutamol as needed. Documented allergy to amoxicillin. Patient was treated with three doses of nebulized salbutamol and IV methylprednisolone. Good clinical response observed with improvement in respiratory status. Patient was discharged home in stable condition. Parents instructed to continue salbutamol as needed and follow up with pediatrician within 48 hours.
aegis_export.corrected_summary: 8-year-old male with known asthma (diagnosed age 5), managed with salbutamol as needed and no current maintenance inhaled corticosteroid, presented to the Emergency Department with an acute moderate-to-severe asthma exacerbation. On arrival, SpO2 was critically low at 88% on room air, indicating severe respiratory compromise. Documented life-threatening drug allergy: Amoxicillin — anaphylaxis at age 6, confirmed by allergist, requiring epinephrine. All penicillin-class antibiotics are explicitly contraindicated. Patient was treated with three consecutive doses of nebulized salbutamol (2.5mg each over 60 minutes), IV methylprednisolone 1mg/kg (28mg), and supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula at 2L/min. Only partial improvement was observed: accessory muscle use decreased but remained present, patient was still tachypneic (RR 28 breaths/min), and persistent mild-to-moderate wheezing continued on auscultation. Post-treatment SpO2 was 93% on supplemental oxygen — still below the target of ≥95%, confirming ongoing respiratory compromise. Clinical assessment confirmed an acute moderate-to-severe asthma exacerbation with incomplete response to initial bronchodilator therapy. Patient was ADMITTED to the pediatric inpatient ward. Inpatient plan includes: continued nebulized salbutamol every 4 hours with reassessment, continuation of systemic corticosteroids (oral prednisolone to follow IV course), continuous SpO2 monitoring with a target of ≥95%, and pediatric pulmonology consult requested for specialist involvement given the incomplete response. Parents were counseled on the necessity of admission and the monitoring plan. DO NOT administer amoxicillin or any penicillin-class antibiotic.
